revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23986660476|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Harachte]][[eo:Horaĥti]][[es:Horajti]][[eu:Horajti]][[fr:Horakhty]][[it:Horakhti]][[ru:Гор-ахти]][[sv:Harakhte]][[uk:Гор-ахті]][[uz:Xoraxte]]
{{Infobox deity |TITEL = Harakhte |IDEOGRAMM = <hiero>G5-N27:X1*Z4</hiero> |NAME = Heru-achti |NAME-ERKLÄRUNG = ''The horizontal {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}'' |TRANSKRIPTION = ''Ḥrw-3ḫtj'' }}
'''Horakhty''' (also '''Hor-akhti''') is in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|en|Egyptian mythology|qid=Q205740|lt=Egyptian mythology|1=Egyptian mythology}} (in the Egyptian {{ill|pantheon|en|pantheon|qid=Q3574594|lt=pantheon|1=Pantheon}}) a manifestation of the god {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}, worshipped specifically as the god of light. This name is Horus of the Two Horizons<ref>https://ancientegyptonline.co.uk/horakhty/</ref>
== Depiction and Significance == Horakhty is depicted as a falcon-headed man wearing the {{ill|solar disk|ja|太陽円盤|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/solar disk|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Was-sceptre|en|Was-sceptre|qid=Q2341121|lt=uraeus|1=Was-sceptre}} on his head.
Horakhty is an early designation for the morning sun.<ref>W. Helck, E. Otto: ''Kleines Lexikon der Ägyptologie.'' Wiesbaden 1999, p. 116.</ref> In the {{ill|Pyramid Texts|en|Pyramid Texts|qid=Q645882|lt=Pyramid Texts|1=Pyramid Texts}}, he is still a deity separate from {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Ra|1=Ra}}. Since the {{ill|Fifth Dynasty of Egypt|en|Fifth Dynasty of Egypt|qid=Q269237|lt=Fifth Dynasty of Egypt|1=Fifth Dynasty of Egypt}}, however, Horakhty has been merged with Ra into {{ill|Re-Hor-achti|en|Re-Hor-achti|qid=Q1418931|lt=Ra-Horakhty|1=Ra-Horakhty}}. In this context, he was identified with Ra, especially with the manifestations of Ra as {{ill|Khepri|en|Khepri|qid=Q215940|lt=Khepri|1=Khepri}} and {{ill|Atum|en|Atum|qid=Q170569|lt=Atum|1=Atum}}, along his daily path across the horizon from east to west. The {{ill|Syncretism (Egyptology)|de|Synkretismus (Ägyptologie)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Syncretism (Egyptology)|lt=syncretic}} deity Ra-Horakhty symbolizes the rising, newly-born {{ill|Sun|en|Sun|qid=Q525|lt=sun|1=Sun}}. The god Horakhty is associated with the morning (rising sun) and the east.
It is suggested that Horakhty, as a specific form of solar belief from {{ill|Heliopolis_(ancient_Egypt)|en|Heliopolis_(ancient_Egypt)|qid=Q191687|lt=Heliopolis|1=Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)}}, significantly contributed to the development of the {{ill|Aten|en|Aten|qid=Q130227|lt=Aten|1=Aten}} cult under {{ill|Akhenaten|en|Akhenaten|qid=Q81794|lt=Akhenaten|1=Akhenaten}} in {{ill|Karnak (disambiguation)|en|Karnak (disambiguation)|qid=Q403182|lt=Karnak|1=Karnak (disambiguation)}}.<ref>Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten.'' Wiesbaden 1995, p. 19.</ref>
== Name == The translation of the name ''Ḥrw-3ḫtj'' (Heru-akhti) is commonly rendered in several variants: "The Horizontal One," "The horizontal Horus," "Horus of the Two Horizons," and "Horus from the Horizon." The reading of the epithet ''3ḫtj'' as a {{ill|Dual (grammatical number)|en|Dual (grammatical number)|qid=Q110022|lt=dual|1=Dual (grammatical number)}} is often translated as "of the two horizons" or "horizontal." Bonnet, however, suggests a more meaningful translation as "Horus of the land of light."<ref>Hans Bonnet: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Hamburg 2000, p. 269.</ref>
== Cult Centers == Horakhty was especially venerated in {{ill|Heliopolis_(ancient_Egypt)|en|Heliopolis_(ancient_Egypt)|qid=Q191687|lt=Heliopolis|1=Heliopolis (ancient Egypt)}} as a local variant of the god Horus. Furthermore, there were cults for him in the {{ill|Precinct of Amun-Re|en|Precinct of Amun-Re|qid=Q1306397|lt=Temple of Amun-Re at Karnak|1=Precinct of Amun-Re}} (under {{ill|Thutmose III|en|Thutmose III|qid=Q157899|lt=Thutmose III|1=Thutmose III}}) and the {{ill|Mortuary_temple_of_Hatshepsut|en|Mortuary_temple_of_Hatshepsut|qid=Q660692|lt=Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|1=Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut}} at {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|ja|デイル・エル＝バハリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Deir el-Bahari|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Along with {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Ra|1=Ra}}, {{ill|Atum|en|Atum|qid=Q170569|lt=Atum|1=Atum}}, and {{ill|Khepri|en|Khepri|qid=Q215940|lt=Khepri|1=Khepri}}, he is invoked in a solar hymn.
== Horakhty and Harmachis == These two local forms of the god Horus differ not only in their hieroglyphic writing but also in their depiction and meaning. {{ill|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harmachis|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, for example, is "Horus '''in''' the horizon," worshipped during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom of Egypt|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}} in the {{ill|Great Sphinx of Giza|en|Great Sphinx of Giza|qid=Q130958|lt=Great Sphinx of Giza|1=Great Sphinx of Giza}}.
== See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian deities|ja|エジプト神の一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Egyptian deities|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Re-Hor-achti|en|Re-Hor-achti|qid=Q1418931|lt=Ra-Horakhty|1=Ra-Horakhty}}
== Literature == * Véronique Berteaux: ''Harachte - Ikonografie, Ikonologie und Einordnung einer Komplexen Gottheit bis zum Ende des Neuen Reiches.'' 2005 (also: Dissertation, Ludwig-Maximilians-University Munich, Munich 2002) * {{ill|Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|ja|ハンス・ボンネ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' 3rd unchanged edition, Nikol, Hamburg 2000, ISBN 3-937872-08-6. * Rolf Felde: ''Ägyptische Gottheiten''. 2nd expanded and revised edition, R. Felde Self-published, Wiesbaden 1995. * {{ill|Wolfgang Helck|en|Wolfgang Helck|qid=Q74888|lt=Wolfgang Helck|1=Wolfgang Helck}}, Eberhard Otto: ''Kleines Lexikon der Ägyptologie''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 1999, ISBN 3-447-04027-0. * Veronica Ions: ''Die Götter und Mythen Ägyptens.'' (= ''The Great Religions of the World - Gods, Myths and Legends.'') Neuer Kaiser Verlag - Buch und Welt, Klagenfurt 1988. * {{ill|Manfred Lurker|ja|マンフレッド・ルルカー|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manfred Lurker|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Lexikon der Götter und Symbole der alten Ägypter.'' Scherz, Bern / Munich / Vienna 1998, ISBN 3-502-16430-4.
== References == <references />
